coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheet nanocomposites sensing platform for sensitive detection of oxalate in urine samples of urolithiasis patients
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Kelin
Chen, Xin
Song, Xuemei
Wu, Yiman
Huang, Ke
Chen, Piaopiao
coredata.dc:description: In the human body, oxalate tends to form calcium oxalate with calcium ions, which can trigger the formation of stones in the urinary system. Therefore, oxalate in urine is usually utilized as a crucial biomarker in clinical urolithiasis diagnoses. In this work, a turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets (NSs) nanocomposites for oxalate sensing in urolithiasis patients. MnO2 NSs is a good sensing platform with high extinction coefficients and rich redox chemistry. The fluorescent N-CDs can be quenched efficiently by MnO2 NSs through the inner filter effect (IFE) to form N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. The reductive oxalate could operate the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ resulting in the dissociation of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites and fluorescence recovery of N-CDs. Under optimal conditions, the developed sensor revealed good specificity toward oxalate with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.69 μM. The developed sensor was successfully applied to quantify oxalate content in 47 urine samples (41 urolithiasis patients and 6 healthy persons). The results showed great consistency with clinical diagnostic reports and computed tomography images. This novel method retains several unique advantages, including affordable, rapid, and validating potential clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxalate
MnO2 nanosheets
Carbon dots
Clinical analysis
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of N-CDs and MnO2 NSs
Feasibility of the N-CDs-MnO2 NSs nanocomposites for oxalate detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21605108 and 82202638), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1090 and 2020YFS0185). We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of N-CDs-MnO2 (A) before and (B) after the reaction with oxalate. (C) XRD comparison chart before and after the reaction. (D) The high resolution XPS spectra of Mn 2p before and after the reaction. (E) Zeta potential values and (F) ICP-MS signal values of the components and mixtures in the detection system. Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.

Feasibility of oxalate detection. (A and B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, MnO2, N-CDs + oxalate, and N-CDs-MnO2 and N-CDs-MnO2 with different concentrations of oxalate. (C) Solutions are described in the sequence above (a. under natural light, b. under 365 nm UV light). (D) UV–vis spectra of the N-CDs and N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, N-CDs-MnO2 + oxalate. Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.

(A) A plot of the relationship between the signal and the concentration of oxalate. (B) Linear relationship between F–F0 and the concentration of oxalate. (C) Interference study of the method for the determination of oxalate (the specially labelled Fe3+, N2H4·H2O, and AA are at the same concentration (300 μM) of oxalate, while the other interference was 10-times concentration (3 mM) of oxalate). Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.

(A) The concentration of oxalate in urine samples of 41 urolithiasis patients and 6 normal people without urolithiasis. (B) CT images of 41 urolithiasis patients (visible stones are indicated by red circles). Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.
Schematic principle of N-CDs-MnO2 NSs for oxalate detection and the process for the actual detection of urine in stone patients.
The comparison of different methods for oxalate detection.

row.entry: TiO2 nanoparticles/multiwalled carbon nanotubes modified electrode
An electrode modified with Fe(III)-tris-(2-thiopyridone) borate

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GNPs-MWCNT: gold colloidal nanoparticles-multi-walled carbon nanotubes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of urine samples for urolithiasis patients and normal people (μM).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheet nanocomposites sensing platform for sensitive detection of oxalate in urine samples of urolithiasis patients

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources
College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610041, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610041, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Kelin Hu and Xin Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the human body, oxalate tends to form calcium oxalate with calcium ions, which can trigger the formation of stones in the urinary system. Therefore, oxalate in urine is usually utilized as a crucial biomarker in clinical urolithiasis diagnoses. In this work, a turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets (NSs) nanocomposites for oxalate sensing in urolithiasis patients. MnO2 NSs is a good sensing platform with high extinction coefficients and rich redox chemistry. The fluorescent N-CDs can be quenched efficiently by MnO2 NSs through the inner filter effect (IFE) to form N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. The reductive oxalate could operate the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ resulting in the dissociation of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites and fluorescence recovery of N-CDs. Under optimal conditions, the developed sensor revealed good specificity toward oxalate with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.69 μM. The developed sensor was successfully applied to quantify oxalate content in 47 urine samples (41 urolithiasis patients and 6 healthy persons). The results showed great consistency with clinical diagnostic reports and computed tomography images. This novel method retains several unique advantages, including affordable, rapid, and validating potential clinical application.

A turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe was developed by carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets (NSs) nanocomposites for oxalate sensing in urolithiasis patients based on the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ by oxalate, resulting in the dissociation of the CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites and fluorescence recovery of CDs.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxalate is a waste product that must be excreted, occurring primarily in the urine. The excess content of oxalate may cause calcium oxalate-stone formation [1]. Urolithiasis is one of industrial society's most common urinary diseases [2]. More than 80% of stones in urolithiasis patient urine were calcium oxalate [3]. The concentration of oxalate (>400 μM) was regarded as one of the most important biomarkers of urolithiasis [4,5]. Therefore, it urgently needs to develop sensitive and selective methods for oxalate assay.

Computed tomography (CT) is usually utilized for stones’ rapid diagnosis [6]. However, it cannot differentiate oxalate from other phosphate stones, and require costly instruments and professional operation. In recent years, high-performance liquid chromatography [7], ion chromatography [8], colorimetric method [9], fluorescent method [10], and electrochemical methods [11] have been developed for sensitive oxalate detection. Among them, fluorescent methods provide additional advantages, including rapid, affordability, and time-saving. Various luminescent substances have been utilized as signal molecules, such as gold nanoclusters, organic dyes, upconversion probes, quantum dots, and carbon dots (CDs) [12,13]. Due to easy preparation, low toxicity, good water solubility, good chemical inertia, and good biocompatibility, CDs was utilized as an ideal nanoprobe in biomolecular imaging and sensing [14,15].

In recent years, MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) has been widely used as a fluorescence quenching agent of fluorescent biosensors due to its strong light absorption in the whole visible range [16,17]. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) may occur when combining MnO2 NSs with fluorescent nanomaterials or probes, which can quench their fluorescence [18–20]. When MnO2 NSs are reduced to Mn2+ by reductive substances like glutathione (GSH), Fe3+, ascorbic acid (AA), etc. [21–24], the fluorescence can be restored and the intensity is in direct proportion to the concentration of these reductive substances. Nano-sensors based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs by MnO2 through the IFE mechanism have been successfully used for direct detection of GSH in cancer cells or human serum and indirect detection of paraoxon in environmental samples [20,25,26]. Therefore, it has great potential to nanocomposite MnO2 NSs and CDs to construct a sensitive, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility for oxalate assay.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and MnO2 nanocomposites were constructed as a turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe to quantify oxalate in 47 clinical urine samples of urinary stones patients. The introduction of dopants could meaningfully improve the photoluminescence behavior of CDs, such as fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and surface state. Nitrogen-doping can further improve the fluorescent performance and stability by adjusting the electronic properties of CDs [27]. The fluorescent N-CDs was efficiently quenched by MnO2 NSs through IFE, while the oxalate could reduce MnO2 NSs to Mn2+, resulting in N-CDs fluorescence recovery (Scheme 1 ). Finally, we extended this sensing platform for oxalate assay in 47 clinical samples. The result obtained by this method agreed with that of clinical diagnosis, indicating the practicability of this platform. This sensing system has the advantages of affordable and sensitive for the quick and precise measurement of oxalate.
All the reagents and apparatus used in this work are detailed in the Supplementary Information (SI).

N-CDs were synthesized by previous works with little modification [28]. One-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize N-CDs using sodium citrate as carbon source and ethylenediamine as heteroatom source. 1.471 g sodium citrate and 335 μL ethylene diamine were mixed into 10 mL ultrapure water, dissolved by stirring, and transferred to a Teflon autoclave before being heated for 5 h at 200 °C. Then it was dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h after cooling. The solution is stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

MnO2 NSs were synthesized from previous literature [29]. In brief, 0.1 M H2SO4 (250 μL), 0.1 M sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) (5 mL), and 44.25 mL ultrapure water were heated at 95 °C for 15 min. Then, 0.05 M (500 μL) KMnO4 solution was added and incubated for 90 min. Then, the synthesized MnO2 NSs were purified by repeated washing with water and ethanol. Brown-black MnO2 NSs powder was obtained after drying. The prepared MnO2 NSs were dispersed in water to form a homogeneous solution and stored at 4 °C.

First, 40 μL of N-CDs (10-fold dilution of the original solution) and 50 μL MnO2 NSs were added to 0.1 M NaAc-HAc buffer (310 μL) and reacted for 15 min. Then, 100 μL oxalic acid with different concentrations was added and reacted for a further 3 min at room temperature. Fluorescence detection was performed immediately with excitation at 340 nm.

Clinical urine samples were provided by the West China Hospital of Sichuan University (Chengdu, China) within 24 h. The study was approved by the Biomedical Ethics Committee of West China Hospital of Sichuan University (approval number: 2018182) and consented by its patients. The whole detection procedure was completed within 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of N-CDs and MnO2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal process from ethylenediamine and sodium citrate. Several characterizations were provided to explore its morphology and properties. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image showed that N-CDs were uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of about 1 nm (Fig. S1A). A characteristic peak of the UV–vis absorption spectrum was observed at 340 nm (Fig. S1B), and the fluorescent radiation was obtained at 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) image of the N-CDs (Fig. S1C) also displayed a broad peak centered at 25°, which was attributed to the highly disordered structure of carbon atoms [28]. In addition, the surface valence state was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The three peaks located at 288.1 eV, 285.9 eV, and 284.6 eV related to C 1s in N-CDs (Fig. S1D), which were attributed to its CO, C–O, and C–C bonds, respectively. In high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s (Fig. S1E), three different bonded related to C–N–C (399.0 eV), N–C (400.5 eV), and N–H (402.0 eV) were observed, respectively [30]. The two peaks of O 1s (530.8 eV and 532.0 eV) corresponded to C–OH and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S1F) [31].

As shown in Fig. S2A, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image showed that a uniform 2D sheet-shaped structure was observed for the prepared MnO2 NSs. In addition, MnO2 NSs have a promising UV–visible absorption spectrum at 370 nm (Fig. S2B). The XRD peaks at 12.3°, 24.5°, 36.7°, 37.3°, and 65.7° were consistent with its standard pattern (No. 86–0666) (Fig. S2C). As shown in XPS spectra (Fig. S2D), the binding energy peak of Mn 2p3/2 is consistent with Mn (IV) (642.5 eV and 643.9 eV) and Mn (III) (641.4 eV). The binding energy at 654.2 eV is attributed to Mn 2p1/2 [32]. All these results indicate the successful preparation of MnO2 NSs.

SEM images were carried out to confirm the composition between the N-CDs and MnO2 NSs. Fig. 1 A showed that spherical N-CDs was located on the lamellar MnO2 NSs structure. When incubated with oxalate, the MnO2 NSs were partially reduced to Mn2+ and the lamellar MnO2 NSs were partially decomposed (Fig. 1B). The XRD spectra of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposite before and after the addition of oxalate were shown in Fig. 1C. The N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites showed an amorphous peak of N-CDs at approximately 30°, and peaks at 13.3°, 23.8° and 42.2° were corresponded to the (003), (006) and (012) crystal planes of MnO2, respectively. After incubation with oxalate, the intensity of the diffraction peak at 23.8° is significantly getting weaker, indicating the morphology dissociation of the MnO2 NSs. The XPS spectra of Mn 2p in the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposite before the reaction showed that the peak of Mn 2p3/2 was divided into two peaks after the reaction, which attributed to Mn (IV) and Mn (II) at 614.6 eV and 641.1 eV, respectively, indicating the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+ (Fig. 1D). To furtherly verify the dissociation of the MnO2 NSs, the Zeta potential of N-CDs, MnO2 NSs, oxalate, N-CDs-MnO2, and N-CDs-MnO2 + oxalate was measured. As shown in Fig. 1E, N-CDs and MnO2 NSs presented negatively charged. After incubation of N-CDs-MnO2 NSs with oxalate, the Zeta potential changed from negative to positive (from −34.6 eV to 12.3 eV) due to the conversion of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. What's more, MnO2 NSs, N-CDs-MnO2, and N-CDs-MnO2 NSs + oxalate (1 and 10 mM) was filtered by 0.22 μM filter membranes, and the Mn2+ content in the filtrate was detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) (as shown in Fig. 1F). The Mn2+ in the filtrate was significantly increased after incubating with oxalate, indicating the degradation of MnO2 NSs (which cannot be intercepted on the membrane). All of the above phenomena demonstrate that the addition of oxalate can effectively reduce the MnO2 NSs in the N-CDs-MnO2 NSs nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the N-CDs-MnO2 NSs nanocomposites for oxalate detection
ce:section.ce:para: Since the absorption spectra of MnO2 NSs partially overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs (as shown in Fig. S1B and Fig. S2B), IFE or FRET may occur. The fluorescence decay curves (Fig. S3) for N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites demonstrated that there was no obvious reduction in the fluorescent lifetime (N-CDs: 3.21 ns; N-CDs-MnO2 NSs: 3.06 ns). As a result, the IFE mechanism should be responsible for the quenching mechanism of N-CDs-MnO2 system [33,34]. Therefore, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by mixing MnO2 NSs. MnO2 can be reduced to Mn2+ by oxalate to inhibit the absorption process causing N-CDs fluorescence intensity (FI) recovery.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the N-CDs revealed strong fluorescence, while pure MnO2 fluorescence has no FI. When MnO2 NSs were introduced, the FI of N-CDs decreased significantly through IFE between N-CDs and MnO2 NSs. As shown in Fig. 2A and B, when oxalate was added, N-CDs fluorescence increased gradually with the increased concentration of oxalate (1–10 mM). It can be observed that the N-CDs-MnO2 NSs nanocomposite fluorescence quenched under natural light and 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 2C). The UV absorption spectra of N-CDs-MnO2 NSs also showed the “on-off” phenomenon of fluorescence after the composition of MnO2 and incubation of oxalate (Fig. 2D), which can also be seen from the fluorescence color change of the reaction solution (Fig. 2C). All the above phenomena indicate that this strategy is feasible to analyze oxalate.

To obtain the optimized conditions, the effects of pH, reaction time, N-CDs, and MnO2 content on this experiment were investigated. The effect of pH was studied in a range of 4–8 for oxalate assay. As shown in Figs. S4A and S4B, the optimal ΔF (F–F0, F: FI after oxalate addition, F0: corresponding FI of the blank N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites) was obtained at pH 5.0. The pH dependence may be involved for two reasons: on the one hand, the reduced system response at lower pH is caused by the instability of MnO2 NSs in acidic media. On the other hand, the oxidation capacity of MnO2 NSs decreases at high pH. As shown in Fig. S4C, the reaction time between N-CDs and MnO2 NSs was investigated, and it was found that the platform was observed after 15 min. Fig. S4D showed that maximum ΔF was obtained at 3 min with oxalate incubation. The similar phenomenon was also obtained by time-dependent response of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites towards oxalate (Fig. S5). In addition, the concentration of MnO2 was also one of the important factors affecting sensing performance. The best signal response occurred when the concentration was 0.3 mg/mL (Fig. S4E). Finally, the effect of adding N-CDs volumes in the range of 20–70 μL was investigated, and Fig. S4F showed that maximum ΔF reached at a volume of 40 μL. Since the reduction of MnO2 NSs by oxalate is an irreversible depletion process, the fluorescent responses of MnO2 NSs and N-CDs prepared from different batches were examined. As can be seen from Fig. S6, a well relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.6% was obtained for the 7 batches of N-CDs-MnO2 NSs nanocomposites for oxalate detection, indicating the proposed system was relatively stable.

Under optimal conditions, Fig. 3 A indicates that the FI increased proportionally with the rise of oxalate concentration (1–300 μM). The calibration curve (Fig. 3B) shows that a satisfied linear relationship was obtained in the range of 1–50 μM with a detection limit (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ and S are the relative standard deviation of 11 blank samples and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively) of 0.69 μM. The linear equation was Y = 9.29C oxalate + 131.29 (R 2 = 0.992). The RSD was calculated to be 2.9% (n = 7). Table 1 compared the analytical performance of this platform with other reported methods for oxalate detection. The result shows that the analytical performance of the proposed method was comparable to those methods.

Selectivity seriously influences the practical application of the proposed sensor. The effect of common existing cations (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and NH4 +), anions (Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, and H2PO4 −) and molecules (N2H4·H2O, AA, bovine serum albumin (BSA), sucrose, urea, glucose, glycine, glutamic acid, valine, and leucine) was investigated to verify the selectivity of the developed oxalate sensor. Fig. 3C revealed that the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites have a high fluorescent response for 300 μM oxalate. However, the signal did not significantly change for other ions and biomolecules at the same concentration or 10-times concentration, indicating that the nanocomposites have strong anti-interference for oxalate sensing. What's more, the effect of the mixture of these substances was also studied. As shown in Fig. 3C, the F–F0 analysis data show that the selective detection of oxalate is hardly affected in the presence of these coexisting substances.

In order to verify the reliability of this method, spiked recovery tests were performed for oxalate analysis in 8 urine samples, and the results are shown in Table S1. The recovery obtained is from 88 to 108%, which proves the practicality of this method for oxalate analysis in actual urine. The t-test was used to validate the significant differences between the added value and determined value of oxalate. The results show that no significant differences were found at the 90% confidence level. The accuracy of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites was validated by using 41 urine samples of urolithiasis patients and 6 normal volunteer samples without urolithiasis. The 400 μM was used as a positive in urine criterion for urolithiasis patients [43]. Fig. 4 A and Table 2 showed that the oxalate concentration in the urine samples of 41 urolithiasis patients was higher than 400 μM, while the concentration in the urine samples of 6 normal people without urolithiasis was less than 400 μM. Table 2 lists the specific concentration of 47 samples, which agree with the clinical imaging results (Fig. 4B). Therefore, the proposed can be applied for oxalate accuracy sensing real urine samples, and its low-cost and rapid capacity can shorten the detection time and cost in clinical diagnosis.

In this study, a simple platform based on the capacity of oxalate to reduce MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ was proposed for oxalate assay via N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. This new bioassay method was low-cost, sensitive, and selective. Fluorescent data show that the oxalate content in the range of 1–50 μM can be successfully assayed by this platform with a low LOD of 690 nM. What's more, oxalate in 47 urine samples including urolithiasis patients and healthy persons can be accurate analysis by this method with great agreement with the clinical CT images. This platform shows considerable advantages in rapid and accurate determination of oxalate. We hope that it can be constituted as a valuable oxalate detection platform and further applied in bioimaging and real-time biosensing. And the sensing strategy also can be extended for other small molecular assay in the future.

Kelin Hu: performed the experiments and wrote the manuscript; Piaopiao Chen and Ke Huang: designed and guided the experiments, and revised the manuscript; Xin Chen: performed the synthesis and characterization of MnO2 NSs and CDs and data processing; Yiman Wu: performed clinical experiments; Xuemei Song: revised the original draft of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21605108 and 82202638), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1090 and 2020YFS0185). We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).
